auction(W, P, B) :- W ∈ winners, P ∈ price, B ∈ butterfly.

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{W1=W2; P1=P2; B1=B2}=0 :- auction(W1,P1,B1), auction(W2,P2,B2), (W1,P1,B1)!=(W2,P2,B2).

% 1. The four butterflies were the swallowtail butterfly, Rena's purchase, the insect that sold for $90, and Phillip's purchase.
% These are four distinct butterflies, so:
% - swallowtail ≠ Rena's purchase
% - swallowtail ≠ $90 item
% - swallowtail ≠ Phillip's purchase
% - Rena's purchase ≠ $90 item
% - Rena's purchase ≠ Phillip's purchase
% - $90 item ≠ Phillip's purchase
% But since each winner has one butterfly, and all are distinct, we can express:
% The swallowtail butterfly is not owned by Rena, not sold for $90, not owned by Phillip.
% Similarly, Rena's purchase ≠ $90 item, etc.

% So:
% swallowtail ≠ Rena's purchase
B!="swallowtail" :- auction(W,P,B), W="Rena".
% swallowtail ≠ $90 item
P!=90 :- auction(W,P,B), B="swallowtail".
% swallowtail ≠ Phillip's purchase
W!="swallowtail" :- auction(W,P,B), W="Phillip".
% Rena's purchase ≠ $90 item
P!=90 :- auction(W,P,B), W="Rena".
% Phillip's purchase ≠ $90 item
P!=90 :- auction(W,P,B), W="Phillip".
% Rena ≠ Phillip → already enforced by uniqueness.

% 2.1 Otis's purchase and the peacock butterfly are different.
B!="peacock" :- auction(W,P,B), W="Otis".
% 2.2 Otis's purchase is the peacock butterfly or the swallowtail butterfly.
{B="peacock"; B="swallowtail"}=1 :- auction(W,P,B), W="Otis".

% 3. Brandon's purchase cost 30 dollars less than Otis's purchase.
P1=P2-30 :- auction(W1,P1,B1), auction(W2,P2,B2), W1="Brandon", W2="Otis".

% 4. Rena's purchase cost 15 dollars less than the swallowtail butterfly.
P1=P2-15 :- auction(W1,P1,B1), auction(W2,P2,B2), W1="Rena", B2="swallowtail".

% 5. The insect that sold for $75 was the torturix butterfly.
B="torturix" :- auction(W,P,B), P=75.
